perturbation.original_text: I/We (Insert name of person or company to whom information is being disclosed) of (Address) hereby agree to keep confidential any information which has already or may be disclosed to us by: - (b) (Inventor’s name) of (c) (Address) Concerning the (d) (Insert brief description of invention) and we will not use it for our own benefit or disclose it to any other party without the written approval of: (b) (Inventor’s name)
perturbation.changed_text: I/We (Insert name of person or company to whom information is being disclosed) of (Address) hereby agree to keep confidential any information which has already or may be disclosed to us by: - (b) (Inventor’s name) of (c) (Address) Concerning the (d) (Insert brief description of invention) and we will not use it for our own benefit or disclose it to any other party without the verbal approval of: (b) (Inventor’s name)
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'written approval' to 'verbal approval' creates ambiguity and potential unenforceability. Written consent provides a clear record, whereas verbal consent is difficult to prove, contradicting standard legal practices for confidentiality agreements.
perturbation.law_citation: 13 Pa. Stat. and Cons. Stat. Ann. § 201
perturbation.law_explanation: The Statute of Frauds typically requires certain agreements, including those involving intellectual property rights, to be in writing to be enforceable. Replacing 'written approval' with 'verbal approval' might render the agreement unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds if it's interpreted to cover the licensing or transfer of significant intellectual property rights, because verbal agreements do not have a paper trail and are hard to confirm.

perturbation.justification: The change from 'written approval' to 'verbal approval' introduces a significant legal contradiction regarding enforceability, as it deviates from standard legal practices, particularly concerning intellectual property agreements. The Statute of Frauds, as mentioned in the Law Explanation, typically requires certain agreements, including those involving intellectual property rights, to be in writing to be enforceable. Although scraped law snippet 1 is missing and snippet 2 is a 'page not found' error, the Law Explanation supports that verbal agreements lack a paper trail and are difficult to confirm, making the modified contract potentially unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds. The Perturbation Explanation reinforces this by highlighting the ambiguity and unenforceability created by relying on verbal consent, which contradicts standard practices for confidentiality agreements.

perturbation.original_text: This obligation of confidentiality and non- use does not apply to information which: 1. Was in our possession before the Inventor disclosed it to me/us. 2. Is made publicly available after its disclosure to me/us other than by any act or omission by us. 3. Becomes known to us after its disclosure by (b) (Inventor’s name) from a third party who is under no obligation of confidentiality to (b) (Inventor’s name)
perturbation.changed_text: This obligation of confidentiality and non- use does not apply to information which: 1. Was in our possession before the Inventor disclosed it to me/us. 2. Is made publicly available after its disclosure to me/us other than by any act or omission by us, unless the information was obtained illegally. 3. Becomes known to us after its disclosure by (b) (Inventor’s name) from a third party who is under no obligation of confidentiality to (b) (Inventor’s name)
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'unless the information was obtained illegally' to exception 2 introduces a contradiction. The legality of information acquisition is irrelevant to the NDA's confidentiality obligation if the information becomes publicly available through no fault of the recipient. Public availability generally negates confidentiality, regardless of how the information initially became available to the public.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA defines trade secrets and outlines protections for them. A key element is that the information must not be generally known or readily ascertainable. The modified clause implies that even if information becomes publicly available (thus losing trade secret status under UTSA), it might still be protected if it was initially obtained illegally, which contradicts the principle that public availability removes trade secret protection.

Union of India & Ors. 2009 (41) PTC 260 (Del.) deliberates on the scope rendered to Sec. 8 (1) of the Patents Act 1970, which reads: (1) Where an applicant for a patent under this Act is prosecuting either alone or jointly with any other person an application for a patent in any country outside India in respect of the same or substantially the same invention, or where to his knowledge such an application is being prosecuted by some person through whom he claims or by some person deriving title from him, he shall file along with his application [or subsequently within the prescribed period as the Controller may allow]— [(a) a statement setting out detailed particulars or such application; and] (b) an undertaking that, [up to the date of grant of patent in India, he would keep the Controller informed in writing, from time to time, of [detailed particulars as required under] clause (a) in respect of every other application - . relating to the same or substantially the same invention, if any, filed in any country outside India subsequently to the filing of the statement referred to in the aforesaid clause, within the prescribed time. 'Chemtura Corporation' had filed a suit seeking to continue an interim injunction granted in their favour for violation of rights in their patent. Chemtura had filed the first application for grant of patent in USA on 27 September 1999, and was granteda patent on 1 August 2006. Correspondingly,
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a condition ('unless the information was obtained illegally') to the exception regarding publicly available information. This contradicts the principle that once information is publicly available, it loses its trade secret status, regardless of how it was initially obtained. The UTSA, as referenced in the Law Explanation, focuses on whether information is 'generally known or readily ascertainable.' The added condition implies that illegal acquisition could negate public availability, creating a contradiction. While the scraped snippets are not directly relevant, the Law Explanation supports the conclusion that the modification contradicts established trade secret law principles.

perturbation.original_text: and we will not use it for our own benefit or disclose it to any other party without the written approval of: (b) (Inventor’s name) This obligation of confidentiality and non- use does not apply to information which: 1. Was in our possession before the Inventor disclosed it to me/us.
perturbation.changed_text: and we will not use it for our own benefit or disclose it to any other party without the written approval of: (b) (Inventor’s name) This obligation of confidentiality and non- use does not apply to information which: 1. Was in our possession before the Inventor disclosed it to me/us, regardless of how it was obtained.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'regardless of how it was obtained' after 'Was in our possession before the Inventor disclosed it to me/us' can be problematic if the prior possession was due to illegal activity (e.g., theft). If the information was obtained illegally before disclosure, it might still be subject to legal restrictions regardless of this NDA.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Economic Espionage Act criminalizes the theft of trade secrets. The modified clause suggests that even if the recipient had illegally obtained the information *before* the disclosure, the NDA's confidentiality obligation would not apply. This could be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent the EEA by legitimizing the use of illegally obtained trade secrets, because illegally obtaining trade secrets is illegal regardless of if the offender had the information beforehand.

Economic espionage (a) In General .-Whoever, intending or knowing that the offense will benefit any foreign government, foreign instrumentality, or foreign agent, knowingly- (1) steals, or without authorization appropriates, takes, carries away, or conceals, or by fraud, artifice, or deception obtains a trade secret; (2) without authorization copies, duplicates, sketches, draws, photographs, downloads, uploads, alters, destroys, photocopies, replicates, transm
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text creates a legal contradiction. The original text states that the obligation of confidentiality does not apply to information that was already in the recipient's possession before disclosure. The changed text adds 'regardless of how it was obtained.' This addition could potentially protect the recipient even if they obtained the information illegally (e.g., through theft or economic espionage) before the disclosure. The Law Explanation states that the Economic Espionage Act (EEA) criminalizes the theft of trade secrets, and the modified clause might circumvent the EEA by legitimizing the use of illegally obtained trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 2 references 18 USC 1831, which concerns Economic Espionage, supporting the idea that illegally obtained trade secrets are subject to legal restrictions.

perturbation.original_text: Is made publicly available after its disclosure to me/us other than by any act or omission by us. 3. Becomes known to us after its disclosure by (b) (Inventor’s name) from a third party who is under no obligation of confidentiality to (b) (Inventor’s name)
perturbation.changed_text: Is made publicly available after its disclosure to me/us other than by any act or omission by us, provided it is deemed in the public interest. 3. Becomes known to us after its disclosure by (b) (Inventor’s name) from a third party who is under no obligation of confidentiality to (b) (Inventor’s name)
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'provided it is deemed in the public interest' introduces a subjective element that contradicts the established legal standard for determining trade secret protection. Trade secret protection is lost when information becomes publicly available, regardless of whether its disclosure is 'in the public interest'.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement of Torts (although not a statute) is a highly influential source of common law regarding trade secrets. Section 757 comment b states that a trade secret must be secret, and that public disclosure destroys the trade secret. The addition of 'provided it is deemed in the public interest' attempts to impose an additional requirement for the loss of trade secret protection that is not recognized in law. Public availability, not public interest, is the deciding factor. Having to confirm if the information is in the 'public interest' introduces subjectivity into what should be a clear rule: If it's public, there's no trade secret, and hence no protection under the NDA.

perturbation.justification: The addition of 'provided it is deemed in the public interest' introduces a subjective criterion to the determination of when information is considered publicly available. This contradicts the legal understanding that public availability itself negates trade secret protection. The Law Explanation notes that the Restatement of Torts states that a trade secret must be secret, and public disclosure destroys the trade secret. The original text aligns with this, while the changed text creates a loophole contingent on 'public interest,' which is not a legally recognized exception to trade secret protection.
